Lecture 10: Constructing Projections ​

Topics: §3.5–3.7 — Uniqueness of Projections, Construction Formula P=B(AB)−1A, Orthogonal Projection Formula P=B(BTB)−1BT, Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling, Least Squares Date: Apr 20–23, 2026

Overview ​

In previous lectures, we learned:

  • Lecture 7: Projection (P2=P), orthogonal projection (P=PT), sunlight-floor model
  • Lecture 8: Cross-filling projections, rank(P)=trace(P), full rank ⟺ invertible
  • Lecture 9: Compatible families, projection decomposition theorem, decomposition methods

Today we solve the construction problem: Given two subspaces W and S, how do we construct a projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S?

Key questions:

  1. Is such a projection unique?
  2. What conditions on W and S guarantee existence?
  3. How do we compute P explicitly?
  4. What is the formula when P is orthogonal?

1. Uniqueness: Projection Determined by Column and Null Space ​

Proposition

Theorem 1.1 (Uniqueness of Projection)

If two projections P and Q satisfy:

Col(P)=Col(Q)andNull(P)=Null(Q)

then P=Q.

Proof:

Step 1: Show P=QP.

Since Col(P)=Col(Q), the interchanging property (Lecture 7) gives:

Col(P)=Col(Q)=Null(I−Q)

Thus (I−Q)P=0, so P=QP.

Step 2: Show Q=QP.

Since Null(P)=Null(Q), the interchanging property gives:

Null(Q)=Null(P)=Col(I−P)

Thus Q(I−P)=0, so Q=QP.

Step 3: Combine.

P=QP=Q

Therefore P=Q. ∎

Remark

Two Subspaces Determine the Projection

A projection P splits the space into two complementary parts: Col(P) (the floor) and Null(P) (the sunlight direction). This theorem says: if you specify which vectors to keep and which to kill, there is exactly one projection doing this job.

2. Bridge Theorem (from Lecture 6) ​

Before constructing projections, we recall a key tool from Lecture 6.

Proposition

Theorem 2.1 (Lecture 6)

For matrices A and B:

Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}⟺Null(AB)=Null(B)

This bridges a geometric condition (subspaces are disjoint) with an algebraic condition (AB has the same null space as B).

3. The Construction Problem ​

Setup: Given two subspaces W and S of Rn, we want to construct a projection P such that:

Col(P)=WandNull(P)=S

Necessary conditions:

  1. W∩S={0} (disjoint — a vector cannot be both on the floor and pointing toward the sun)
  2. dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=n (complementary — every vector decomposes uniquely)

Strategy: Express the subspaces using matrices.

  • Column space (constructive form): W=Col(B), where B is n×r with full column rank (r=dim⁡(W))
  • Null space (descriptive form): S=Null(A), where A is r×n with full row rank

The product AB is r×r (square).

Proposition

Theorem 3.1 (AB Invertible Condition)

With notation as above:

W∩S={0}⟺AB is invertible

Proof:

W∩S={0} means Null(A)∩Col(B)={0}.

By the bridge theorem (Theorem 2.1): Null(AB)=Null(B).

Since B has full column rank, Null(B)={0}. Therefore Null(AB)={0}, which means AB has linearly independent columns. Since AB is r×r (square), this gives rank(AB)=r. By Theorem 4.4 of Lecture 8: AB is invertible. ∎

4. Construction Formula ​

Proposition

Theorem 4.1 (Construction of Projection)

Suppose:

  • W=Col(B) where B is n×r with full column rank
  • S=Null(A) where A is r×n with full row rank
  • W∩S={0} (equivalently, AB is invertible)

Then the unique projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S is:

P=B(AB)−1A

Proof:

We verify three things.

P2=P:

P2=B(AB)−1A⋅B(AB)−1A=B(AB)−1(AB)(AB)−1A=B(AB)−1A=P✓

Col(P)=Col(B):

Since P=B(AB)−1A, every column of P is a linear combination of columns of B, so Col(P)⊆Col(B).

For the reverse: PB=B(AB)−1(AB)=B. So every column of B is in Col(P). ✓

Null(P)=Null(A):

If Ax=0: Px=B(AB)−1Ax=0. So Null(A)⊆Null(P).

If Px=0: B(AB)−1Ax=0. Since B has full column rank: (AB)−1Ax=0. Multiply by AB: Ax=0. So Null(P)⊆Null(A). ✓

By uniqueness (Theorem 1.1), this is the unique such projection. ∎

Remark

Understanding the Formula

The formula P=B(AB)−1A has a clear structure:

  • A on the right: "test" whether the input is in Null(A)=S
  • (AB)−1 in the middle: rescaling factor (needed to make P2=P)
  • B on the left: output vectors in Col(B)=W

Example

Example 4.1: Projection in R3

Let:

W=span{(101)},S=span{(10−1),(010)}

Step 1: dim⁡(W)=1, dim⁡(S)=2, dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=3 ✓

Step 2: Check disjointness. A vector in W: (a0a). A vector in S: (bc−b). Equal implies a=b, 0=c, a=−b, so a=0. ✓

Step 3: B=(101), A=(101) (since S satisfies x1+x3=0).

Step 4: AB=(101)(101)=2, so (AB)−1=12.

Step 5:

P=B(AB)−1A=(101)⋅12⋅(101)=12(101000101)

Verify: P2=P ✓, Col(P)=W ✓, P⋅(10−1)=0 ✓, P⋅(010)=0 ✓

5. Orthogonal Projection Formula ​

For an orthogonal projection onto W, we want Null(P)=W⊥.

From Lecture 6 (Four Fundamental Subspaces), if W=Col(B), then:

W⊥=Null(BT)

Therefore, in the construction formula, we set A=BT.

Proposition

Theorem 5.1 (Orthogonal Projection Formula)

Let B be an n×r matrix with full column rank (rank r). The orthogonal projection onto Col(B) is:

P=B(BTB)−1BT

Proof:

By the construction formula with A=BT:

P=B(BTB)−1BT

BTB is invertible: Suppose (BTB)x=0. Then xT(BTB)x=(Bx)T(Bx)=∥Bx∥2=0. So Bx=0. Since B has full column rank: x=0. Therefore Null(BTB)={0}, so BTB is invertible (by Lecture 8, Theorem 4.4).

P=PT: Since BTB is symmetric, so is (BTB)−1:

PT=(B(BTB)−1BT)T=B((BTB)−1)TBT=B(BTB)−1BT=P✓

Therefore P is an orthogonal projection by Lecture 7, Theorem 4.1. ∎

Example

Example 5.1: Orthogonal Projection onto a Line

Project orthogonally onto W=span{(12)} in R2.

B=(12), BTB=1+4=5, (BTB)−1=15.

P=15(12)(12)=15(1224)

Verify: P=PT ✓, P2=P ✓

Example

Example 5.2: Orthogonal Projection onto a Plane

Project orthogonally onto W=span{(100),(010)} (the xy-plane in R3).

B=(100100), BTB=I2, (BTB)−1=I2.

P=(100100)I2(100010)=(100010000)

Verify: P(xyz)=(xy0) (projects onto xy-plane) ✓

6. Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling ​

From Lecture 9, the inner cross-filling method decomposes a projection by cross-filling an inner factor. For orthogonal projections written as P=B(BTB)−1BT, we can cross-fill the small r×r matrix (BTB)−1 instead of the large n×n matrix P.

Proposition

Theorem 6.1 (Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling)

Let P=B(BTB)−1BT be an orthogonal projection. Decompose (BTB)−1 by diagonal cross-filling:

(BTB)−1=Q1+Q2+⋯+Qr

where each Qj is rank-1 and symmetric (diagonal pivots preserve symmetry).

Define Rj=BQjBT. Then:

  1. P=R1+R2+⋯+Rr
  2. Each Rj is a rank-1 orthogonal projection
  3. {R1,…,Rr} is a compatible family

Proof:

(1): ∑Rj=∑BQjBT=B(∑Qj)BT=B(BTB)−1BT=P. ✓

(2): Since rank(Rj)≤rank(Qj)=1 and ∑rank(Rj)≤r=rank(P), the projection decomposition theorem (Lecture 9, Theorem 3.1) gives: each Rj is a projection.

For symmetry: RjT=(BQjBT)T=BQjTBT=BQjBT=Rj (since QjT=Qj).

So each Rj is an orthogonal projection. ✓

(3): Follows from the projection decomposition theorem. ✓ ∎

::: success Efficiency Gain

MethodMatrix sizeCost
Direct diagonal cross-filling of Pn×nO(n2) per step
Inner diagonal cross-filling of (BTB)−1r×rO(r2) per step

When r≪n, the inner method is much faster. :::

Example

Example 6.1: Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling

Let B=(101112) (3×2, full column rank).

Step 1: BTB=(3335), det=15−9=6.

(BTB)−1=16(5−3−33)

Step 2: Diagonal cross-fill at (1,1) with pivot 56:

Q1=15/6⋅16(5−3)⋅16(5−3)=16(5−3−39/5)Q2=(BTB)−1−Q1=16(0006/5)=15(0001)

Step 3: Compute R2=BQ2BT:

BQ2=(101112)15(0001)=15(000102)R2=BQ2BT=15(012)(012)=15(000012024)

Check: R2=R2T ✓, rank(R2)=1 ✓, trace(R2)=1 ✓

R1=P−R2 is the other rank-1 orthogonal projection.

But we haven't yet extracted the real treasure from this decomposition.

6.1 Finding Orthogonal Bases: The Real Payoff ​

Each Qj from the diagonal cross-filling is rank-1 and symmetric. Because the pivot is on the diagonal, Qj has the form:

Qj=1qjjdjdjT

where dj is the column at the pivot position and qjj is the pivot value.

Now compute BQj:

BQj=1qjj(Bdj)djT

This tells us: BQj is rank-1, and its column space is spanned by Bdj.

Therefore:

Rj=BQjBT=1qjj(Bdj)(Bdj)T

Attention

The Key Insight

Each rank-1 orthogonal projection Rj projects onto the line spanned by Bdj.

Since {R1,…,Rr} is a compatible family with RiRj=0, their column spaces are mutually orthogonal. Therefore:

Bd1,Bd2,…,Bdr

are mutually orthogonal vectors that span Col(B).

To find an orthogonal basis for Col(B): just read off dj from each Qj and multiply by B.

In practice, we don't even need to compute the full matrices Qj or Rj. Since Qj is rank-1, all of its information is in one column. We just need:

  1. Diagonally cross-fill (BTB)−1: at each step, read off the column at the pivot position → dj
  2. Compute Bdj → orthogonal direction
  3. Normalize if needed: vj=Bdj/∥Bdj∥ → orthonormal basis vector

Remark

No Gram-Schmidt Needed!

The classical Gram-Schmidt process finds an orthogonal basis by iteratively subtracting projections:

  • Start with b1
  • Subtract the projection of b2 onto b1 to get v2
  • Subtract projections of b3 onto v1,v2 to get v3
  • And so on...

The inner diagonal cross-filling method is fundamentally different:

  • Compute (BTB)−1 (an r×r matrix)
  • Cross-fill it diagonally (reading off columns)
  • Multiply each column by B

The orthogonal directions emerge simultaneously from the cross-filling — no iterative subtraction required. The mutual orthogonality is guaranteed by the projection decomposition theorem.

Example

Example 6.2: Extracting the Orthogonal Basis (Continuing Example 6.1)

We had B=(101112) and (BTB)−1=16(5−3−33).

Step 1: Cross-fill at diagonal pivot (1,1) with value 56.

Read off column 1 of (BTB)−1: d1=16(5−3), or equivalently d1∝(5−3).

Step 2: Compute Bd1:

B(5−3)=(101112)(5−3)=(52−1)

Step 3: Remainder after cross-filling: Q2=15(0001).

Read off the nonzero column: d2∝(01).

B(01)=(012)

Step 4: Verify orthogonality:

(52−1)T(012)=0+2−2=0✓

Result: {(52−1),(012)} is an orthogonal basis for Col(B).

Verify: The original basis was {(111),(012)}. Indeed:

5⋅(111)+(−3)⋅(012)=(52−1)∈Col(B)✓

The cross-filling told us exactly which linear combination of the original columns produces an orthogonal basis — the coefficients are in dj!

::: success Summary: Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling for Orthogonal Bases

Given a basis {b1,…,br} for a subspace W (columns of B):

  1. Compute (BTB)−1 (an r×r symmetric matrix)
  2. Diagonally cross-fill (BTB)−1: at each step, read the column dj at the pivot
  3. Compute Bdj — this is an orthogonal basis vector for W

What dj tells you: the coefficients for combining the original columns of B into mutually orthogonal vectors. The cross-filling of (BTB)−1 computes these coefficients automatically.

Normalize vj=Bdj/∥Bdj∥ if an orthonormal basis is desired. :::

7. Application: Least Squares ​

The orthogonal projection formula provides an elegant solution to the least squares problem.

Proposition

Theorem 7.1 (Least Squares)

Given an inconsistent system Ax=b (where A is m×n with full column rank), the least squares solution x^ that minimizes ∥b−Ax∥ satisfies:

ATAx^=ATb

and the best approximation is Ax^=Pb where P=A(ATA)−1AT is the orthogonal projection onto Col(A).

Proof:

The vector Ax^∈Col(A) that is closest to b is the orthogonal projection of b onto Col(A):

Ax^=Pb=A(ATA)−1ATb

Left-canceling A (which has full column rank):

x^=(ATA)−1ATb

Equivalently: ATAx^=ATb. ∎

Remark

Why "Least Squares"?

The residual b−Ax^=(I−P)b is orthogonal to Col(A) (since I−P projects onto Col(A)⊥). This is the geometric meaning of "least squares": the closest point in a subspace is found by dropping a perpendicular.

8. Summary ​

::: success Key Results from This Lecture

  1. Uniqueness (§1): A projection is uniquely determined by Col(P) and Null(P).

  2. Construction Formula (§4):

P=B(AB)−1A

where Col(B)=W, Null(A)=S, and W∩S={0} (equivalently, AB invertible). Here B(AB)−1 and (AB)−1A are left/right inverses of each other.

  1. Orthogonal Projection Formula (§5): Set A=BT to get:
P=B(BTB)−1BT

  1. Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling (§6): Decompose (BTB)−1 instead of P — much more efficient when r≪n. Normalizing the columns produces an orthonormal basis for Col(B) without Gram–Schmidt.

  2. Cross-Filling the Inverse (§6): Cross-fill the augmented matrix (A∣I) with pivots in A — the inverse A−1 emerges in the right half as a by-product.

  3. Extending to Orthonormal Basis (§6): Cross-fill both P and I−P to obtain a complete orthonormal basis for Rn — all by cross-filling.

  4. Least Squares (§7): The least squares solution is x^=(ATA)−1ATb. :::

Looking Ahead ​

With projection theory complete, we now have all the tools needed for spectral decomposition (Chapter 5).

The idea: if a matrix A can be written as A=λ1P1+λ2P2+⋯+λkPk where {P1,…,Pk} is a compatible family, then:

  • An=λ1nP1+⋯+λknPk
  • eA=eλ1P1+⋯+eλkPk
  • Any function f(A)=f(λ1)P1+⋯+f(λk)Pk

Finding the λi (eigenvalues) and Pi (spectral projections) is the goal of Chapters 4–5.

Exercises ​

Problem

Exercise 1

Find the projection P onto W along S where:

W=span{(110)},S=span{(1−10),(001)}

Verify P2=P, Col(P)=W, and Null(P)=S.

Problem

Exercise 2

Let P be the orthogonal projection onto W=span{(34)} in R2.

(a) Use the formula P=B(BTB)−1BT to compute P.

(b) Verify P=PT and P2=P.

(c) Compute the orthogonal projection of b=(50) onto W.

Problem

Exercise 3: Orthogonal Basis via Inner Diagonal Cross-Filling

Let W=span{(101),(011)} in R3.

(a) Compute BTB and (BTB)−1.

(b) Diagonally cross-fill (BTB)−1 into Q1+Q2.

(c) Read off the column dj from each Qj. Compute Bd1 and Bd2.

(d) Verify that Bd1 and Bd2 are orthogonal. You have found an orthogonal basis for W without Gram-Schmidt!

(e) Normalize to get an orthonormal basis.

Problem

Exercise 4

Suppose B is n×r with full column rank, and A is r×n with full row rank, satisfying AB=Ir.

(a) Show that P=BA is a projection with Col(P)=Col(B) and Null(P)=Null(A).

(b) How does this relate to the formula P=B(AB)−1A?

Problem

Exercise 5: Least Squares

Consider the inconsistent system Ax=b where:

A=(101112),b=(088)

(a) Compute ATA and ATb.

(b) Solve the normal equation ATAx^=ATb.

(c) Compute Pb where P=A(ATA)−1AT and verify Pb=Ax^.

Problem

Exercise 6

True or false: If W and S are subspaces with dim⁡(W)+dim⁡(S)=n, then there always exists a projection P with Col(P)=W and Null(P)=S.

If false, give a counterexample. If true, prove it.
